title: Cat Sneezing Fits: Causes, Symptoms, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat having sneezing fits? Learn what causes these episodes, why the urgency level is usually low, and how photos help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Short sneezing fits in cats are typically harmless reflexes to environmental irritants, though you should seek veterinary care if sneezing is accompanied by respiratory distress or thick discharge.

What causes a short sneezing fit in cats?

A short sneezing fit in cats is a sudden, rapid succession of sneezes, often occurring when the nasal passages become irritated. This is a natural reflex designed to expel foreign particles, such as dust, pollen, or strong fragrances. In many cases, these fits are transient and resolve once the irritant is cleared from the airway.

When is my cat's sneezing considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. Most sneezing fits are not emergencies and do not require immediate veterinary intervention.
  • Monitor your cat: Keep a close watch on your pet for any worsening symptoms.
  • Schedule a routine appointment if: Sneezing becomes chronic, is accompanied by thick discharge, or if your cat stops eating.
  • Seek urgent care if: Your cat is experiencing visible respiratory distress or significant nasal bleeding.

How can a photo or video of my cat sneezing help the vet?

  • Visual Documentation: Recording a video of the episode or taking a clear photo of nasal discharge provides essential information for your veterinary team.
  • Clinical Observation: These visuals allow the vet to observe the nature of the sneeze and the characteristics of any fluid.
  • Accurate Triage: Seeing the symptoms helps differentiate between simple environmental allergies and more complex upper respiratory infections.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, sneezing fits are most frequently associated with acute rhinitis and feline viral rhinotracheitis (FVR), characterized by frequent sneezing, nasal discharge, conjunctivitis, and rhinitis, often accompanied by salivation and fever that may fluctuate. Excitement or movement can induce sneezing. Nasal discharge starts as serous but may become mucoid or mucopurulent due to secondary bacterial infections. An aspiration reflex, characterized by a short paroxysmal episode of inspiratory effort, may also be observed as the cat attempts to clear the nasopharynx. Dual viral infections or concurrent infections with organisms like *C felis*, *Mycoplasma* spp, reoviruses, or *Bartonella henselae* may further complicate the condition. Transmission occurs via aerosol droplets and fomites.

Chapter: Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1481)

faqs.question: Can environmental allergies cause sneezing in cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats can be sensitive to dust, mold, and seasonal pollens, which often manifest as sudden sneezing fits.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is sneezing frequently?
faqs.answer: Observe your cat for other symptoms like eye discharge or lethargy. If the sneezing persists for more than 24 hours, contact your vet for a check-up.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to sneezing?
faqs.answer: Brachycephalic breeds, like Persians, may be more prone to respiratory issues and sneezing due to their unique facial structure.
